w:r.w:t: Design, Validation, and Handover Narrative for the Site 2 Environment Supporting Company 1, Company 2, and MSP Operations
Formal technical handover and operations report
Site 2 services supporting Company 1, Company 2, and MSP operations
Client IT staff, MSP support teams, and successor operators
Company stakeholders, MSP operations, and successor support teams
Internal technical handover and operational review record
Bailey Kulla, Elyazid Sidelkheir, Ru Wang, Justin Rosseleve, Yiqin Huang, Omer Deniz

This report explains the Site 2 architecture, operational logic, current operating state, and handover-ready support context using design inputs, environment evidence, official vendor documentation, and observed operating results collected for the Site 2 deployment.

This report is written as a professional technical document for formal review, operational handover, and client-facing interpretation. Presentation flow and walkthrough guidance are maintained separately.

This document is written in a client/MSP handover style while retaining the formal structure expected in a formal technical handover document, including a title page, table of contents, executive summary, introduction, discussion, conclusion, appendices, and IEEE-style references.
Site 2 logical service inventory and platform role map
OPNsense interfaces, aliases, and limited edge exposure
OPNsense OpenVPN and inter-site rule mapping
Company 1 services from the Site 2 management path
Active Directory, DNS, and DHCP evidence
Active Directory, DNS, and DHCP evidence
iSCSI-backed storage and mounted volume evidence
SMB share definitions and synchronization evidence
repository, backup jobs, and offsite-copy evidence
Design inputs and evidence basis for Site 2
Evidence classes used in this report
Observed Site 2 systems and service roles
Site 2 logical service inventory and role mapping
OPNsense exposure, routing, and firewall policy summary
Company 2 identity, DNS, and DHCP summary
Storage, backup, and recovery data-flow summary

Site 2 operates as an integrated service environment that combines MSP administration, Company 1 services, Company 2 services, isolated storage, and backup or recovery workflows within a single managed design. Rather than being explained one server at a time, it is best understood as a coordinated platform in which routing, identity, storage, web delivery, and protection services all contribute to the final operating model.
This report is based on three evidence classes:
design requirements and service objectives for the Site 2 environment
the Site 2 environment evidence set, including gateway configuration evidence, logical inventory records, and SAN screenshots
current operating observations recorded on March 23 and March 24, 2026
Current analysis shows Site 2 operating through three tightly related service layers:
MSP management through controlled jump hosts, OPNsense segmentation, and an OpenVPN-backed inter-site path
The most important technical findings are:
the OPNsense configuration shows deliberate segmentation across MSP,
, and a Site 1 OpenVPN path, while exposing only the jump hosts at the WAN edge
were healthy for Samba AD and DHCP, and both held the expected dual A records for
operate within a shared forest; the observed cross-domain DNS visibility and client behavior are consistent with that design
showed a live iSCSI session to
share definitions, and successful Site 1 to Site 2 synchronization
, confirming that the same hostnames work from both company perspectives
available backup design evidence indicates that

protects 10 machines to a Site 2 dedicated disk, stores file-share backup data, and copies those backups offsite to Site 1; current operating observations align with the expected management and control paths to the Veeam server
Taken together, the available configuration evidence, observed service behavior, and vendor-supported design characteristics present a coherent and supportable Site 2 operating model.
The purpose of this document is to describe Site 2 in a form that supports operational handover, ongoing support, and formal service review.

This version is grounded in the available design inputs, the current Site 2 evidence set, official vendor documentation for the deployed technologies, and current operating observations recorded on March 23 and March 24, 2026.

The report deliberately follows a formal handover structure so that it can be reviewed by technical leads, client stakeholders, and successor operators without requiring a separate explanatory walkthrough. The emphasis is on design rationale, operating state, and service relationships rather than on build notes alone.
For web-service discussion, the report is intentionally limited to the internal service hostnames used by the environment:
The previously discussed external hostname is intentionally excluded from the technical narrative because it is outside the required scope of this package.
The evidence set establishes two things at the same time: what Site 2 is expected to deliver as an operational environment, and what can be proven directly from the current implementation.
At the design level, Site 2 is expected to provide:
MSP-controlled administrative entry through bounded jump-host access
service delivery for both Company 1 and Company 2 within the same operating site
segmented networking, site-to-site communication, and limited edge exposure
SMB-backed file services with isolated iSCSI-based storage transport
backup and offsite-protection capability that is operationally separate from live service delivery
The current documentation set includes direct environment evidence for Site 2:
gateway configuration evidence containing the OPNsense interface, NAT, alias, routing, and firewall configuration
a platform inventory record identifying the Site 2 system set and service roles
a confirmed role mapping that identifies
as the Company 1 client role for Site 2
a confirmed namespace relationship showing that
a confirmed backup-design note stating that Veeam protects 10 machines to a Site 2 disk, retains file-share backup data, and copies that protected set offsite to Site 1
Table 1. Design inputs and evidence basis for Site 2
Site 2 must support MSP operations, Company 1, Company 2, storage, backup, and secure connectivity
Site 2 must be documented as an integrated multi-service environment rather than as a single workload
Company 1, Company 2, and MSP responsibilities coexist inside one operating site
The technical narrative must explain how all three scopes interact
Real interface, NAT, alias, route, and firewall logic for Site 2
Network and security sections can be evidence-based rather than inferred
Current system set and service-role distribution
The system inventory and role mapping can be presented without exposing hypervisor tooling
Exact isolated-storage addressing and gateway structure
The storage architecture can be explained as a deliberate transport design
Shared-forest interpretation and Company 1 client-role mapping
Cross-domain reachability and client behavior can be described accurately
Protected workload count, file-share backup, and offsite-copy intent
The protection narrative can distinguish backup from synchronization and live service delivery
Actual observed behavior through approved administrative paths
The final conclusions can be supported by current operational evidence
2.2 Evidence classes used in this report
This report separates evidence by source so that claims remain accurate and supportable.
Table 2. Evidence classes used in this report
Service requirements, service-role mapping, confirmed role notes, and backup design summary
Defines intended scope, operating model, and service expectations
Gateway configuration evidence, platform inventory record, SAN screenshots, and confirmed environment notes
Provides architecture details that are valid even when not always directly queryable over CLI
OPNsense, Samba, Ubuntu Server, Microsoft IIS, and Veeam documentation
Supports the rationale for network policy, identity, storage, web delivery, and protection design
March 23 and March 24, 2026 observations from the approved remote path, jump hosts, MSP bastion, and low-impact CLI review
Describes current operating state without configuration changes
2.3 Evidence-based method used for this report
This report was rebuilt using only:
the available Site 2 design inputs and evidence records
official documentation for the core technologies deployed in Site 2
current operating observations recorded on March 23 and March 24, 2026
The observation approach followed a management-first bastion model.

was treated as the primary bastion for low-risk CLI review into Company 1 and Company 2 paths. The Windows jump and local workstation were used only where they added practical reachability context. Low-impact CLI review against selected Linux systems was limited to state confirmation without configuration changes.
To verify controlled access to Site 2 jump services through the authorized operator path
To perform low-risk CLI review from the MSP management plane into Company 1 and Company 2 paths
To add Windows-jump reachability context and external operator perspective where needed
Low-impact CLI review of Linux systems
To confirm service state without configuration changes
To fill architecture details that are accurate but not always directly queryable from CLI

Site 2 should be read as a complete service site, not as a loose grouping of servers. It combines MSP administrative control, Company 1 cross-site service delivery, Company 2 core tenant services, isolated storage transport, and backup or recovery functions inside one routed operating model. That whole-site view matters because the design only makes sense when those layers are evaluated together.

The current operating observations and the configuration evidence support that whole-site reading clearly. MSP control is represented by OPNsense, the jump systems, and Veeam. Company 1 is represented by its directory, file, client, web, and storage services. Company 2 is represented by its identity, file, client, and web services. Together, those components define the real operating boundary of Site 2.
Table 4. Observed Site 2 systems and service roles
OPNsense MSP gateway and segmentation point
Windows jump on the MSP segment
Linux jump on the MSP segment
, identified in the environment evidence as the Company 1 client role
internal Company 1 web service on the Site 2 path
Company 2 file service and storage consumer
, isolated storage segment for Company 1 file services
, isolated storage segment for Company 2 file services
Figure 1. Site 2 topology and service-role alignment.
Description: Insert a topology or diagram view showing MSP jump paths, OPNsense, Company 1 services, Company 2 services, isolated SAN paths,
, and the logical relationship between the major service groups.
Insert Figure 1 here in the final document.
3.2 Site 2 Logical Service Inventory and Platform Roles

The system inventory matters because it demonstrates planned platform structure rather than ad-hoc implementation. Site 2 was built as a separated service estate with clear roles for MSP control, Company 1 delivery, Company 2 operations, storage transport, and protection services. That role-mapping view is the foundation for understanding dependency, failure domain, and support ownership.
One useful example is the Company 1 Linux client role. Earlier environment records identify this role as
, while the current IP and hostname evidence labels the same function as
in the narrative while retaining that alias for traceability.
Table 5. Site 2 logical service inventory and role mapping

This platform layout is useful because it demonstrates role separation, shows intentional infrastructure design, and makes the environment easier to understand for an operations handoff without exposing the underlying training or hosting platform.
Figure 2. Site 2 logical service inventory and platform role map.
Description: Insert a sanitized logical inventory figure, role map, or architecture view that identifies the major Site 2 systems and their functions without exposing hypervisor-management tooling.
Insert Figure 2 here in the final document.
3.3 MSP Entry, Network Segmentation, Remote Access, and Security Rationale

The network layer is the controlling discipline of Site 2. If routing, segmentation, and exposure control are not well designed, then identity, storage, web delivery, and backup quickly become difficult to defend. The available OPNsense configuration evidence shows that Site 2 applies a disciplined bastion-first model rather than relying on broad edge exposure.
The OPNsense interface layout is structured as follows:
The WAN NAT rules expose only the jump systems:

That limited edge exposure is significant. It means the environment is designed around entering through bastion hosts and then performing controlled internal administration, which is consistent with standard supportable network operations practice.
Table 6. Site 2 network segments and gateways
Jump hosts, OPNsense, and Veeam access plane
Company 1 web and DMZ publishing segment
Company 2 web and DMZ publishing segment
Isolated SAN path used by Company 1 storage
Isolated SAN path used by Company 2 storage
Site 1 to Site 2 routing, backup, and cross-site access
The firewall and alias design is equally instructive. The configuration evidence defines aliases for
. That alias-driven style makes the policy easier to maintain and easier to explain in a handover report.
Table 7. OPNsense exposure, routing, and firewall policy summary
Reduces attack surface and matches bastion-host guidance
OpenVPN interface plus Site 1 route for
Supports cross-site service reachability and backup copy flow
Company 1 can reach shared services without flat access to Company 2 networks
Company 2 can reach shared services without flat access to Company 1 networks
Each site can reach the other site’s web service using the expected hostname path
Supports shared name resolution across the service environment
Supports offsite backup transfer between the two sites
Current operating observations matched this design well:
Approved remote management access to both jump services was successful
, which is consistent with a present but non-anonymous management plane
Figure 3. OPNsense interfaces, aliases, and limited edge exposure.

, aligned to the active Company 1 Linux client role
These components cover directory services, file services, client services, internal web services, and isolated storage.

successfully resolved and reached both required web hostnames

Present in the environment inventory evidence

Naming behavior is consistent across the two company contexts

Insert Figure 5 here in the final document.
3.5 Company 2 Identity, Shared Forest Context, DNS, and DHCP
The identity layer is one of the strongest and most operationally important parts of Site 2. Current observations on
valid DNS records for both Company 1 and Company 2 web services on both nodes

These checks directly satisfy multiple service requirements. Company 2 requires directory services, DNS, and fault-tolerant DHCP. The current operating state shows that those services remain queryable and usable through the approved bastion path.

share a forest. That forest relationship was not reconfigured during testing, so it is treated as environment design evidence. The live results are consistent with that design because both Company 1 and Company 2 web namespaces are visible on Company 2 identity servers and are reachable from both clients.
Table 9. Company 2 identity, DNS, and DHCP summary

The presence of Company 1 records on the Company 2 identity servers is especially important. It shows that Site 2 was designed to support cross-site internal web delivery rather than keeping each company’s namespace invisible from the other side.
Active Directory, DNS, and DHCP evidence.
showing active Samba AD, active DHCP, and DNS query output for the two web hostnames.
Insert Figure 6 here in the final document.
Active Directory, DNS, and DHCP evidence.
showing active Samba AD, active DHCP, and DNS query output for the two web hostnames.
Insert Figure 7 here in the final document.
Figure 8. Shared-forest and cross-domain DNS evidence.

Description: Insert a screenshot that best demonstrates the shared-forest or cross-domain namespace design, such as dual web records on Company 2 identity services or a domain-management view that shows both domains.
Insert Figure 8 here in the final document.
3.6 Storage, File Services, and Isolated SAN Design

The storage layer is one of the clearest indicators that Site 2 was engineered rather than improvised. The environment uses isolated SAN connectivity, mounted block storage, structured share presentation, and synchronization behavior in a way that aligns cleanly with the project’s storage objectives.
successful synchronization from Site 1 to Site 2 in the current log
The SAN evidence clarifies an architectural layer that CLI testing on
alone would not fully explain. Two isolated SAN servers are present in the design:

Those SAN systems are isolated and bridged only to the corresponding file servers. That is a strong design choice because it keeps storage traffic off the tenant LAN and DMZ segments while still satisfying the iSCSI requirement in a supportable way. Administrative-path checks from
did not expose the SAN endpoints as general-purpose management services, which is consistent with the intended isolated-storage model rather than a flat routed design.
Table 10. Storage and isolated SAN summary
Two isolated storage bridges support the two file-service domains without flattening storage into routed user segments
This is one of the strongest sections of the Site 2 design because the architecture, the supporting evidence, and the current operating observations all reinforce the same conclusion.
iSCSI-backed storage and mounted volume evidence.
Description: Insert a screenshot showing the active iSCSI session and the mounted

SMB share definitions and synchronization evidence.

share definitions and the successful synchronization log entries.

IP configuration and demonstrates the isolated Company 1 storage segment.

IP configuration and demonstrates the isolated Company 2 storage segment.

3.7 Client Access, Identity Validation, and Dual-Hostname Web Delivery

Client-side observation is essential because a service environment is only meaningful when directory, web, and file outcomes are visible from real consumer systems. For Site 2, the client perspective shows that the design is not only correctly configured on servers, but also usable from both company contexts.
Two different client perspectives were available:
, identified in the environment evidence as the Company 1 client role
Both clients were able to resolve and reach both internal web hostnames. That result matters because it proves the same named services can be consumed consistently from both company perspectives:
additionally showed Company 1 realm membership and identified itself under
realm membership and valid domain-user lookups for
Table 11. Client access and identity summary
Proves Company 1 can consume both tenant web names
Proves Company 2 can consume both tenant web names
The bastion-side curl tests also showed a hardened web pattern:
That hostname-first behavior shows that the services are published intentionally, not exposed as loose IP-based pages.
membership plus successful access to both
Insert Figure 13 here in the final document.
, domain-user lookup, and successful access to both required web hostnames.
Insert Figure 14 here in the final document.

Backup and recovery must be documented at the same level of care as identity or networking because they define whether the environment is supportable after failure. In Site 2, the available design evidence and the current operating observations complement each other well.
According to the available backup design evidence:
those backups are written to a Site 2 dedicated disk on the Veeam server
a file-share backup also exists on the Veeam server
the environment also sends offsite backup data to Site 1
The OPNsense configuration evidence supports that overall story by defining:
Management-path checks corroborated the management and control plane by showing that
was reachable from the approved admin path on:
OPNsense alias plus live port checks
Management and agent ports are healthy from the bastion path
Site 2 has a dedicated Veeam backup disk
File-share backup exists on the Veeam server
Environment design note plus OPNsense route and rule evidence
Offsite backup design is part of the deployed architecture
10 machines are included in Veeam protection

This is enough to document a strong backup architecture, but not enough to overstate GUI evidence such as exact job names or repository labels. Those should be closed with one Veeam console screenshot in the final package.
repository, backup jobs, and offsite-copy evidence.
Description: Insert a screenshot from the Veeam console showing the protected machines, the Site 2 repository or disk, the file-share backup entry, and the offsite-copy relationship to Site 1.
Insert Figure 15 here in the final document.

Requirement traceability is what separates a persuasive technical report from a simple build summary. In Site 2, each major requirement needs to be tied to a concrete implementation choice and to live or documentary evidence that proves it was delivered.
, approved remote operator access, and OPNsense WAN NAT only for jump systems
Bastion reachability checks plus gateway NAT review
Edge exposure remains limited to jump services only
Configuration review plus current path checks
Support with one OPNsense GUI screenshot
Route or rule review plus Company 1 reachability from Site 2
Use rule screenshots if a reviewer asks how traffic is controlled
running Samba AD, DNS, and DHCP
Pair with screenshots from both nodes
Bastion and client curl checks returning
Inventory evidence, administrative-path checks, client access, DNS visibility, and SAN evidence
Document the current service set and operating state
Add one interactive share screenshot for maximum polish
initiator, isolated storage network, mirrored storage logic
Distinguish storage transport from user LAN traffic in the technical explanation
resolving and reaching both required hostnames
Direct client-side web and identity checks
Confirms the current user-side service experience from both company contexts
Backup, file-share backup, and offsite copy
, Site 2 repository disk, file-share backup, and Site 1 offsite-copy design
Live port checks, route or rule review, and available backup design detail
Needs one Veeam GUI screenshot for full closure
Linux client reachable through the approved management path
Internal SSH/admin path visible from Site 2
If a reviewer requests explicit external-edge evidence, provide a current live test or screenshot from that access path
The matrix below aligns the stated service requirements with the corresponding environment evidence.
3.10 Service Dependency, Failure Domains, and Access Model

Site 2 contains several distinct service planes, and each one fails differently. Mapping those dependencies makes troubleshooting faster and reduces the risk of treating a symptom as though it were the root cause.
Table 15. Service dependency and failure-domain view
Team cannot reach consoles or internal hosts quickly
Approved remote-access verification and bastion reachability checks
Client logon, hostname resolution, share access, cross-domain name visibility
User lookup fails or hostnames do not resolve
Hostname fails or wrong content is served
VM recovery, file-share backup, offsite resilience
Backup copy or restore confidence drops
Port checks plus Veeam console screenshot
Administrative-path reachability, client behavior, and environment inventory evidence
Company 1 service paths are unavailable from the expected operating flow
checks, hostname curl, inventory review, and client-side checks
Jump hosts, OPNsense, Veeam, and approved internal admin path
Centralizes privileged access through bastion systems
Company 1 systems through the approved administrative path
Preserves tenant scoping while allowing approved administrative access
User authentication and normal service consumption
Reflects normal tenant use of Company 1 services
Keeps tenant administration aligned to Company 2 services
Demonstrates that directory services produce real client outcomes
Group-based public access and per-user private isolation
Reinforces least privilege and private-home-folder behavior
Hostname-based delivery prevents accidental raw-IP exposure
Backup monitoring, repository review, and recovery operations
Recovery should be manageable without logging into every tenant server
This view improves the handover quality because it explains not only where services live, but which identities are meant to operate them.

The data-protection model in Site 2 should be read as three different but related mechanisms: synchronized file content, SAN-backed storage delivery, and Veeam-based backup with offsite copy. Keeping those flows distinct is important because each solves a different operational problem and each requires different evidence.
Table 17. Storage, backup, and recovery data-flow summary
Site 1 synchronized content and local file operations
Sync workflow and mounted iSCSI-backed volume
Useful for operational data continuity and backup-backed restore
Site 1 synchronized private content and local per-user changes
Sync plus access control plus backup
Preserves per-user isolation during restore scenarios
Base dependency for all file-service presentation
Isolated bridge to Company 1 file services
Keeps Company 1 storage domain separate from Company 2
10 protected machines per available backup design
Supports recovery beyond what sync alone can provide
File services included in backup plan
Provides resilience against a Site 2-local loss event

The key operational lesson is that synchronization is not the same thing as backup. The sync job helps keep file content aligned between sites, while Veeam provides recovery-oriented protection and offsite resilience. Both need to be present in the final explanation.

Operations guidance is part of technical completeness, not an optional appendix. A supportable Site 2 report has to identify which routine checks actually preserve service continuity and why those checks matter.
Without the bastion path, many healthy services become slow to prove
Review OPNsense interface and tunnel status
Routing and inter-site access are central to the Site 2 story
Identity and DHCP are foundational for Company 2
Reconfirm Company 1 and Company 2 DNS records on Company 2 identity nodes
Cross-domain name visibility is central to the Site 2 service narrative
Confirm both clients can still resolve both web hostnames
This is one of the most visible cross-site service behaviors
iSCSI session and mount remain present
Storage issues can break both file access and service availability
Cross-site synchronization is part of the Site 2 narrative
control ports and last repository/offsite state
Backup readiness depends on stable control and copy paths
Confirm Company 1 and Company 2 hostnames still return
Reinforces hostname-based publishing and catches unexpected web-path regressions
Maintain an up-to-date evidence set for OPNsense, storage, and Veeam
Keeps the handover package ready for audit and operational review
Table 19 summarizes the primary fault domains and first-line checks for common service symptoms.
Table 19. Troubleshooting and fast triage guide
Approved remote-access path and jump hosts
Authorized remote-access path, edge publication, or jump-host issue
DNS service, record replication, or client resolver issue
Realm visible and user entries returned
SSSD, domain join, or identity-service issue
SMB active, mount present, iSCSI session visible
SMB service, mount loss, or SAN transport issue
Client, jump host, and DNS path
by hostname and by raw IP
Wrong hostname, DNS mismatch, or web virtual-host issue
Company 1 no longer appears reachable from Site 2
Ports and Company 1 web path succeed
OpenVPN/rule path or remote Company 1 service issue
Backup or offsite-copy confidence is low
Port checks, static-route review, and Veeam GUI
Veeam reachable and copy path defined
Veeam service, repository, or inter-site route/rule issue
Management, tenant service delivery, storage, and protection workflows are represented across the environment
Design inputs, inventory evidence, current observations, and requirement traceability
Segmentation, bounded entry, inter-site routing, and service-specific policy behavior are consistent across the environment
OPNsense configuration evidence, NAT mappings, interface definitions, aliases, route logic, and current reachability checks
Identity, client access, file services, storage transport, and backup paths are aligned with the documented operating model
Samba AD and DHCP state, DNS records, client access,

When the design inputs, environment evidence, official technical references, and current operating observations are read together, Site 2 is best understood as a complete operating environment supporting Company 1, Company 2, and MSP services together.
Site 2 currently shows strong evidence for:
controlled entry through bastion hosts and tightly limited edge exposure
deliberate OPNsense segmentation across MSP, Company 1, Company 2, DMZ, and inter-site paths
Company 1 services including directory, file, web, client, and SAN roles
healthy Company 2 identity, DNS, and DHCP services
isolated SAN-backed storage with live iSCSI consumption and synchronized file services
two-client access confirming that both required web hostnames work from both company perspectives
Veeam-based backup design with Site 2 repository use and Site 1 offsite-copy intent
explicit requirement traceability and troubleshooting context that make the environment easier to hand over and defend

Taken as a whole, Site 2 is defined less by any single tenant than by the way its management, identity, storage, web, and recovery layers operate together. That integrated behavior is the correct basis for the final technical narrative.
